summary: Judea Pearl outlines the paradigmatic shift required to move from traditional associational statistics to causal inference. He introduces the Structural Causal Model (SCM), the do-operator for simulating interventions, and graphical tools like the back-door criterion to identify causal effects from non-experimental data.

principles.statement: Causal and associational concepts do not mix.
principles.rationale: A joint distribution function only describes data under static conditions; it contains no information about how the distribution would change if external conditions or treatments were modified.
principles.source_quote: This distinction implies that causal and associational concepts do not mix; there is nothing in a distribution function to tell us how that distribution would differ if external conditions were to change...

principles.statement: Probability calculus is insufficient for causal analysis.
principles.rationale: Standard probability syntax is symmetric and cannot express directional causal facts, such as the reality that symptoms do not cause diseases.
principles.source_quote: This distinction further implies that causal relations cannot be expressed in the language of probability and, hence, that any mathematical approach to causal analysis must acquire new notation – probability calculus is insufficient.

principles.statement: Every causal claim must rely on untested causal assumptions.
principles.rationale: Unlike associational assumptions, causal assumptions cannot be verified from observational data alone without experimental control. They must be provided by theoretical or judgmental premises.
principles.source_quote: Every claim invoking causal concepts must rely on some premises that invoke such concepts; it cannot be inferred from, or even defined in terms statistical associations alone.

frameworks.name: General Methodology for Causal Inference
frameworks.when_to_apply: When systematically tackling problems of causal inference to move from assumptions to estimated effects.
steps: Define the causal problem and target quantities.
Assume the causal relationships and structural model (e.g., using a causal diagram).
Identify whether the target causal quantity can be computed from the observed distribution given the assumptions.
Estimate the identified causal quantity from the data.
frameworks.source_quote: Section 4 outlines a general methodology to guide problems of causal inference: Define, Assume, Identify and Estimate...

frameworks.name: The Back-door Criterion
frameworks.when_to_apply: When selecting a sufficient or admissible set of covariates to adjust for in order to estimate the causal effect of X on Y from observational data.
steps: Ensure no element of the selected set S is a descendant of the treatment variable X.
Ensure the elements of S 'block' all 'back-door' paths from X to Y (i.e., all paths that end with an arrow pointing to X).
frameworks.source_quote: A set S is admissible (or "sufficient") for adjustment if two conditions hold: 1. No element of S is a descendant of X. 2. The elements of S "block" all "back-door" paths from X to Y...

mental_models.name: The do(x) Operator (Intervention as Equation Deletion)
mental_models.description: A mathematical operator that simulates physical interventions by deleting the structural equations of the intervened variables and replacing them with constants, while keeping the rest of the model unchanged.
mental_models.example: To emulate an intervention do(x0) that holds X constant in a model, replace the equation for X with X = x0 to obtain a new, post-intervention model.

mental_models.name: Causal Diagrams (Path Diagrams)
mental_models.description: A graphical representation where arrows denote possible causal influences, and missing arrows or missing double-arrows represent empirical claims of zero influence or zero covariance.
mental_models.example: Drawing an arrow from Disease to Symptom, but omitting the arrow from Symptom to Disease, visually and mathematically encodes the assumption that symptoms do not cause diseases.

heuristics: An associational concept can be defined by a joint distribution; a causal concept cannot.
In path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded not in the links but, rather, in the missing links.

signature_quotes.text: This distinction implies that causal and associational concepts do not mix; there is nothing in a distribution function to tell us how that distribution would differ if external conditions were to change...
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the fundamental demarcation line between standard statistics and causal inference.

signature_quotes.text: The syntax of probability calculus does not permit us to express the simple fact that “symptoms do not cause diseases,” let alone draw mathematical conclusions from such facts.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing for the necessity of new mathematical notation for causal analysis.

signature_quotes.text: In path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded not in the links but, rather, in the missing links.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how to read and interpret structural causal models.

signature_quotes.text: Every claim invoking causal concepts must rely on some premises that invoke such concepts; it cannot be inferred from, or even defined in terms statistical associations alone.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting the necessity of untested causal assumptions.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to answer causal questions from data or distributions alone without causal assumptions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The laws of probability theory do not dictate how one property of a distribution ought to change when another property is modified by external intervention.

anti_patterns.description: Interpreting structural coefficients merely as regression coefficients.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Regression coefficients are artifacts of analysis that are by definition uncorrelated with regressors. Structural coefficients represent actual physical/causal effects and retain their meaning regardless of whether error terms are correlated.

anti_patterns.description: Using standard probability calculus to express causal relations.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Probability calculus is symmetric and cannot distinguish statistical dependence (e.g., P(disease|symptom)) from directional causal dependence.
